perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall safeguard Proprietary Information using at least the same degree of care the Receiving Party uses to protect its own proprietary information, but with no less than reasonable care under the circumstances.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall attempt to safeguard Proprietary Information.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces a defined standard of care ('at least the same degree of care...but with no less than reasonable care') with a vague obligation ('attempt to safeguard'). This makes the obligation unenforceable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arizona law regarding trade secrets requires reasonable efforts to maintain secrecy. The original text ensures a minimum standard of 'reasonable care.' The modified text, by only requiring an 'attempt' at safeguarding, could be interpreted as less than 'reasonable efforts,' thus failing to meet the standard required to maintain trade secret protection under Arizona law. This makes the proprietary information vulnerable to disclosure and use, undermining the purpose of the NDA.

The official version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is published by Thomson Reuters. ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 44 Next Section Printable Version 44-401 - Definitions 44-401 . Definitions In this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires: 1. "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy or espionage through electronic or other means. 2. "Misappropriation" means either: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means. (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who either: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret. (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, was acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use or was derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use. (iii) Before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. 3. "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency or any other legal or commercial entity. 4. "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique or process, that both: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use. (b) Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a legal contradiction by weakening the required standard of care for protecting trade secrets under Arizona law. The original text mandates 'at least the same degree of care...but with no less than reasonable care,' ensuring a specific level of protection. The modified text, requiring only an 'attempt to safeguard,' introduces ambiguity and potentially falls short of 'reasonable efforts' to maintain secrecy. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and 2 both define 'trade secret' partly by whether it 'is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' By reducing the standard to a mere 'attempt,' the modified text undermines the effort needed to legally define and protect a trade secret, potentially leading to its misappropriation, as defined in the snippets, because the 'reasonable efforts' condition is no longer clearly met.

perturbation.original_text: Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, unless othe rwise promptly requested by the Disclos ing Party in advance of such expiration or termination, the Receiving Party shall return or destroy (and , in the case of destruction, promptly provide a certification of such destruction to the Disclosing Party) all Proprietary Information in its possession , including , without limitation, any copies, summaries, and/or compilations made of or from Proprietary Information, and shall make no further use of Proprietary Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon expiration or termination of this Agreement, the Receiving Party should consider returning or destroying Proprietary Information in its possession.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text replaces the mandatory obligation to return or destroy proprietary information with a suggestion. This makes the obligation discretionary.
perturbation.law_explanation: A.R.S. § 44-404(A) addresses remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. By making the return/destruction of proprietary information discretionary, the modified text weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to seek remedies under Arizona law for misappropriation. If the Receiving Party only 'considers' returning or destroying the information, they may continue to use it, potentially violating the Disclosing Party's trade secret rights, with fewer clear legal recourse options available to the Disclosing Party due to the ambiguous nature of the obligation to return or destroy.

Attorney fees The court may award reasonable attorney fees to the prevailing party for any of the following: 1. A claim of misappropriation made in bad faith. 2. A motion to terminate an injunction made or resisted in bad faith. 3. Willful and malicious misappropriation. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the Receiving Party to return or destroy Proprietary Information, whereas the changed text suggests the Receiving Party should 'consider' doing so. This creates an ambiguous obligation. The Law Explanation states that this weakens the Disclosing Party's ability to seek remedies under Arizona law (A.R.S. § 44-404(A)) for misappropriation. Scraped Law Snippet 2, citing Arizona Revised Statutes § 44-404 (2022), discusses attorney fees in cases of misappropriation. While this snippet focuses on attorney fees, the Law Explanation connects the weakening of the obligation with potential difficulties in seeking remedies for misappropriation, making the weakening of the obligation significant, and thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Without regard to the duration of its other obligations hereunder, the Receiving Party shall control access to, and use of, Proprietary Information and the direct product thereof in accordance with all applicable foreign and U.S. export laws and regulations, including, without limitation , the Arms Export Control Act and the International Traffic in Arms Regulations (ITAR), 22 C.F.R. parts 120 - 130 and the Export Administration Act and the Export Administration Regulations (EAR), 15 C.F.R. parts 730 - 774 (uExport Laws and Regulations") .
perturbation.changed_text: Without regard to the duration of its other obligations hereunder, the Receiving Party should endeavor to control access to, and use of, Proprietary Information in general compliance with foreign and U.S. export laws and regulations.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall control...in accordance with' with 'should endeavor to control...in general compliance' weakens the obligation. The specific references to ITAR and EAR are also removed, reducing clarity.
perturbation.law_explanation: 22 CFR § 123.22 mandates specific record-keeping and reporting requirements for exports controlled under ITAR. By changing 'shall control' to 'should endeavor to control' and removing the specific reference to ITAR, the modified text makes compliance discretionary. This means the Receiving Party may not adhere to the mandatory record-keeping and reporting requirements, potentially violating ITAR regulations. The term 'general compliance' introduces further ambiguity, making it unclear what specific actions are required to meet the standard.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 22 CFR § 123.22 - Filing, retention, and return of export licenses and filing of export information. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 22 CFR § 123.22 - Filing, retention, and return of export licenses and filing of export information. CFR prev | next § 123.22 Filing, retention, and return of export licenses and filing of export information. (a) Any export, as defined in this subchapter, of a defense article controlled by this subchapter, to include defense articles transiting the United States, requires the electronic reporting of export information. The reporting of the export information shall be to the U.S. Customs and Border Protection using its electronic system(s), or directly to the Directorate of Defense Trade Controls (DDTC), as appropriate. Before the export of any hardware, via a license or other authorization, the DDTC registered applicant/exporter, or an agent acting on the filer's behalf, must electronically submit export information to U.S. Customs and Border Protection, unless electronic reporting is unavailable, in which case U.S. Customs and Border Protection will issue instructions ( see paragraph (b) of this section). In addition to electronically providing the export information to U.S. Customs and Border Protection before export, all mandatory supporting documentation (e.g., attachments, certifications, proof of filing in U.S. Customs and Border Protection's system(s) such as the Internal Transaction Number (ITN)) must be submitted electronically, unless electronic reporting of such information is unavailable, in which case U.S. Customs and Border Protection will issue instructions. (1) If necessary, an export may be made through a port other than the one designated on the license if the exporter complies with the procedures established by U.S. Customs and Border Protection. (2) When a defense article is temporarily exported from the United States and
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to control access to Proprietary Information. The original text states the Receiving Party 'shall control...in accordance with' export laws, while the changed text states the Receiving Party 'should endeavor to control...in general compliance'. This change introduces ambiguity and makes compliance discretionary, contradicting the mandatory nature of export regulations. Scraped Law Snippet 2, 22 CFR § 123.22, specifies mandatory electronic reporting requirements for exports. By weakening the control obligation and removing specific references to ITAR/EAR, the modified text undermines the mandatory reporting and record-keeping required under ITAR, creating a legal contradiction as the party may not adhere to the mandated regulations, or potentially claim that 'general compliance' was met without full adherence.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and interpreted under the internal laws of the state of Arizona . U.S.A.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be subject to the laws of Arizona, U.S.A.
perturbation.explanation: The original text specifies Arizona law as governing. The modified text makes the governing law uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: A.R.S. § 12-101 establishes the jurisdiction of Arizona courts. By replacing 'shall be governed by' with 'may be subject to', the modified text introduces uncertainty as to which jurisdiction's laws apply. This could lead to disputes over which court has jurisdiction and what legal principles govern the interpretation of the contract, potentially hindering the enforcement of the agreement in Arizona courts. It implies that another jurisdiction's laws could also apply, contradicting the intent of specifying Arizona law as the governing law.

ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 12 Next Section Printable Version 12-101 - Justices; number; term; election 12-101 . Justices; number; term; election The supreme court shall consist of seven justices. The term of office of each justice shall be six years and shall take effect on the first Monday in January of the year in which it begins. Justices shall be elected at the general election next preceding the expiration of a term of office. perturbation.justification: The original text uses "shall be governed by," which is a definitive statement. The changed text replaces this with "may be subject to," introducing ambiguity regarding which laws govern the agreement. This directly contradicts the original intent to specify Arizona law. While the provided law snippets discuss the number and election of justices (A.R.S. § 12-101), the Law Explanation clarifies the implication that uncertainty in governing law can lead to disputes over jurisdiction and enforcement, essentially undermining the original contract's clarity and enforceability under Arizona law. The change introduces an ambiguity that makes the original obligation uncertain, fitting the definition of an Ambiguity Legal Contradiction.
